mappings.rationaleAI: Historiography from Herodotus and Thucydides through Ranke, the Annales school, and postmodern challenges directly traces the development of historical writing (A.1) across classical (A.1.a.ii), Enlightenment (A.1.c.iii), and 19th-20th century periods (A.1.c.iv).

Thucydides' methodology emphasising empirical observation and rejection of mythological explanation is foundational for classical historiography (A.1.a.ii), and his concept of human nature as constant relates to factors in historical writing (A.3).
Gibbon's History of the Decline and Fall exemplifies Enlightenment historiography (A.1.c.iii) and illustrates factors in writing history including the author's rationalist vantage point (A.3).
Ibn Khaldun's Muqaddimah pioneered sociological analysis of history, making him the preeminent figure in Islamic historiography (A.1.d.i).
Sources of early Chinese history including oracle bones, Sima Qian's Records, and bamboo texts address East Asian historiography (A.1.d.ii) and sources for historical writing (B.1).

Hegel's philosophy of history with its dialectical method and concept of progressive consciousness of freedom is a major speculative philosophy of history (C.2) and a foundational conception of the philosophy of history (C.1).
The idea of progress from Enlightenment optimism through postmodern skepticism represents a central speculative philosophy of history concerning the pattern of historical events (C.2).
The origins of writing from cuneiform to alphabetic script cover the development of written materials as sources for historical writing (B.1) and connect to epigraphy (B.2.f).
The Phoenicians' revolutionary alphabetic writing system connects to Near Eastern historiography (A.1.a.i) and linguistics as an auxiliary discipline (B.2.l).
Bede's Ecclesiastical History established the AD dating system, connecting to Western Christian historiography (A.1.b.i) and chronology as an auxiliary discipline (B.2.d).
Cicero's philosophical works transmitting Greek thought to Rome and his influence on Renaissance humanism connect to classical historiography (A.1.a.ii).
Strabo's Geographica as a comprehensive description of the known world connects to classical historiography (A.1.a.ii) and geography as an auxiliary discipline (B.2.i).
Al-Biruni's comparative study of Indian civilization in his Kitab al-Hind represents a pioneering example of Islamic historiography (A.1.d.i).
